serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of 12 baked oysters with garlic miso topping, weighing approximately 300 g in total including the miso-garlic sauce and fried garlic garnish.

nutrient_source.title: Oysters, eastern, wild, cooked, dry heat (USDA SR Legacy #15169) combined with miso paste (USDA SR Legacy #16112) and garlic (USDA SR Legacy #11215); composite estimation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for baked oysters with garlic miso topping. Nutrient values are estimated by compositing USDA SR Legacy data for dry-heat cooked eastern oysters (~70% of dish weight), white miso paste (~15%), butter (~8%), and fried garlic (~7%). Energy cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central entries. Values represent a reasonable per-100g estimate for the combined dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; shellfish and seafood category; oysters analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for baked oysters with garlic miso. Oysters are a protein- and mineral-rich shellfish with minimal digestible carbohydrate. A GI of 25 is estimated by analogy with other low-GI shellfish and seafood entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. Miso paste contributes fermented soy carbohydrates with a low GI (~30 for soy-based fermented products). The composite GI of 25 is defensible and conservative. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (25 × 22.2) / 100 = 5.6 (carbohydrate per 300 g serving = 7.4 × 3 = 22.2 g).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from miso paste and seasoning (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1260 mg per 12-piece serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 4–6 pieces per occasion, as the miso-based topping contributes substantial sodium (~1260 mg per full serving), which may approach or exceed the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~25) and moderate carbohydrate content (~7.4 g per 100 g) with high protein supporting glycemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Baked oysters with garlic miso have a low glycemic index of approximately 25 and a glycemic load of 5.6 per 12-piece serving, making them a suitable occasional choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, provided sodium intake from the miso topping is monitored concurrently.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Oysters provide cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids and zinc, but miso and butter contribute saturated fat and sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While oysters are a source of omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids and zinc that support cardiovascular health, the miso-garlic-butter topping adds saturated fat (~2.1 g per 100 g) and high sodium (~420 mg per 100 g), so individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish in moderation and request reduced butter preparation where possible.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Baked Oysters with Garlic Miso (12 Pieces) is a Japanese-style baked shellfish dish consisting of fresh oysters baked in their half-shells and topped with a savoury garlic miso paste, finished with crispy fried garlic bits. The dish is served as a sharing platter of 12 pieces and is a signature offering at Japanese oyster bars and izakaya-style restaurants in Singapore, such as The Oyster Bank at Funan Mall. The preparation involves coating shucked oysters with a blend of white miso (shiro miso), minced garlic, butter, sake, and mirin, then baking at high heat until the topping is caramelised and the oyster is just cooked through. The result is a rich, umami-forward flavour profile with a creamy, slightly smoky texture from the miso glaze and a crunchy contrast from the fried garlic garnish. Nutritionally, oysters are one of the most nutrient-dense shellfish available, providing exceptional levels of zinc (~39 mg per 100 g), vitamin B12 (~8.5 mcg per 100 g), selenium (~54 mcg per 100 g), and iron (~4.8 mg per 100 g). The dish delivers approximately 118 kcal per 100 g with 9.8 g protein, 5.2 g fat, and 7.4 g carbohydrate. The glycemic index is estimated at 25 (low), making it suitable for blood glucose management. The primary clinical concern is sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g) contributed by the miso paste, which is relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is not halal due to the shellfish content and potential alcohol in sake and mirin. It contains soy (miso), gluten (miso), and shellfish allergens.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this exact dish (baked oysters with garlic miso topping). Nutrient values are estimated by compositing USDA SR Legacy data for dry-heat cooked oysters, white miso paste, butter, and garlic. The composite estimation is methodologically sound but introduces uncertainty. GI is an analogous estimate based on shellfish and fermented soy entries in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked eastern oysters (FNDDS #15169), white miso paste (#16112), butter (#01001), and raw garlic (#11215). No single approved-source record exists for this exact preparation. 2. Serving size of 300 g for 12 pieces is estimated based on typical oyster half-shell weights (approximately 20–25 g edible meat per oyster) plus miso-garlic topping weight. 3. Sodium content is notably elevated due to miso paste; actual sodium may vary by restaurant recipe. 4. GI of 25 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. 5. The dish is not halal due to shellfish and alcohol (sake, mirin) in the preparation. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy composite and are not independently verified for this specific restaurant preparation. 7. Price range (SGD 35.07–68.66) preserved from input; this wide range likely reflects different oyster grades or set menu inclusions at The Oyster Bank - Funan. 8. cuisine_tag 'Alcoholic Drink' from input is noted but not carried forward as a primary classification; sake and mirin are cooking ingredients, not the primary product category.
